title: "An endless demand for compute" | Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq
summary: Groq is a hardware chip specifically designed for LLM inference. Jonathan Ross, the founder, discusses Groq's LPU architecture, its comparison to GPUs via Pareto curves and cost-per-token metrics, and a strategic agreement with NVIDIA. The interview covers technical aspects like static scheduling and mixture-of-experts models.
writeup: Jonathan Ross, who pioneered Google's TPUs before founding Groq, explains how the Language Processing Unit (LPU) architecture addresses a specific bottleneck: GPU inference is memory-bound because it relies on slow external DRAM, forcing large batch sizes that hurt latency. The LPU instead spreads models across chips using fast on-chip SRAM and uses static compile-time scheduling, achieving lower cost-per-token at high speeds where GPUs underperform. The conversation traces from the original Google compute scarcity that sparked the TPU, through how static scheduling accommodates mixture-of-experts routing, to why real-time audio quality actually improves on faster hardware by enabling larger chunk sizes.

key_points: The original motivation for the TPU was a 2013 Google speech recognition model that beat human transcription accuracy but could not be deployed at scale, being limited to Nexus phone users because Google lacked sufficient inference compute.

GPUs are memory-bottlenecked at inference time because they pull expert weights from external DRAM and must amortize that cost across large batch sizes, sometimes hundreds of queries; the LPU uses on-chip SRAM so a batch size of around 10 is economically viable, cutting queuing latency.

Static compile-time scheduling does not mean fixed data: the LPU uses scatter-gather operations to fetch whichever expert is needed at runtime, so mixture-of-experts models run correctly while still benefiting from the deterministic timing guarantees that prevent pipeline stalls.

On the Pareto curve of cost-per-token versus speed, GPUs dominate the slow high-throughput end, LPUs dominate the fast low-latency end, and combining both fills the middle zone to give the best cost at any desired speed.

Speech-to-text quality improves on LPU hardware because the chip processes audio hundreds of times faster than real time, allowing much larger audio chunks per inference call; smaller chunks force the model to predict words without full sentence context, raising error rates.

Algorithmic flexibility matters more than peak specialization: a recent algorithmic change improved attention efficiency roughly 10x, so a chip locked to one architecture would immediately lose that gain, and the LPU is deliberately designed for easy reprogramming as new architectures emerge.
